author: The founder
body:
My water broke three weeks early. It happened while I was at work, and I wasn't even completely sure that's what it was. I waited to see if contractions would start, but when they didn't after a day, I called the clinic. They asked me to come in for some tests.

It was a Thursday evening. After a few hours in the emergency room, they confirmed that my water had broken and told me I wouldn't be going home until the baby was born. We were completely unprepared. I called my husband to meet me at the hospital. After they told us I had to stay, he went home to get a few things, including the hospital bag we still hadn't packed.

I felt overwhelmed. There were things at work I hadn't finished, and I wasn't ready for labor to begin. The staff at the hospital wanted to start the induction right away because of the risk of infection. I wanted to wait and give my body some time, but things quickly became chaotic. Each new shift brought new staff with new opinions. One of the attending doctors was very pushy and insisted we skip some steps. Later, after the shift changed, a nurse quietly mentioned that the approach they were using seemed unusual. At the time, I was too stressed and confused to fully take it in.

That first night and through the next day, they kept increasing the Pitocin dose. I was having contractions, but I couldn't feel them. They performed several cervical checks. At first, things seemed to be progressing a little, but not by much. By early Saturday morning, they told me they had reached the maximum level of Pitocin and that I was 4 centimeters dilated. They paused the induction and told me to rest.

That was when I started doing some research. I realized that, according to standard guidelines, my body hadn't even been ready for Pitocin in the first place. I brought it up with the staff, and they admitted that a mistake might have been made. Still, they believed they could continue and make it work.

They restarted the induction that morning, giving me one dose of misoprostol before switching back to Pitocin, again without checking my cervix first. When they finally did another exam, they said I was only 2 centimeters dilated. I felt confused and frustrated. Then the shift changed again, and a new doctor came in and said we should seriously consider a C-section due to the growing risk of hemorrhage.

I hadn't slept in two nights. I was emotionally and physically drained. I could barely speak. I cried and felt completely disconnected. Nothing was going the way I had hoped. I had planned for a natural birth with minimal interventions and as little hospital time as possible. Now I was stuck in a situation I didn't recognize and couldn't control.

They asked me to decide whether I wanted to wait and try again after a pause or proceed with a C-section. Even among the staff, there was disagreement. I asked for one more cervical exam before deciding. This time they said I was only 1 centimeter dilated. That was when I started shaking uncontrollably, and I finally began to feel the contractions more strongly.

My husband and I decided that the safest option for the baby was to go ahead with the C-section. I thought we would have a little time, but as soon as we agreed, everything happened quickly. They rushed me into the operating room. I was cold, shaking, and couldn't stop crying. They asked me to sit still for the epidural, but I physically couldn't stop shaking.

I kept thinking about how much I had wanted the baby to be born naturally. Everything felt like it was moving without me. I felt like I was losing control of my body. I may have blacked out for a moment. I remember hearing them talking to my husband about the baby. She was born quickly. They brought her to me for just a few seconds, placed her on my chest, and then took her away with my husband.

There had been a hemorrhage. They were struggling to stop the bleeding. My mind was foggy and fading in and out. I asked a nurse why it was taking so long. She said there were complications and it's taking a bit longer.

Eventually, after a few hours, they brought me to the recovery room where my husband and the baby were waiting. At that point, the only thing I could focus on was that she was alive and safe with him. I honestly thought I might not make it, but seeing them together gave me some comfort. I told myself that if anything happened to me, at least she would be taken care of.

During those first hours, and maybe even the entire first day, I felt completely disconnected. I couldn't move freely. The baby wasn't with me most of the time (even though I really wanted to hold her). They would only bring her when it was time to feed. She wasn't doing well. My husband kept telling the nurses that she seemed cold and needed warmth, but no one suggested just placing her on my chest. They told me to rest. I wish I had insisted on holding her. I regret not keeping her close.

At one point, the nurses said she was breathing too fast and took her for tests. I was stuck in our room, unable to get up, just calling them and asking when they would bring her back.

After the first day, they removed the catheter and the tube that was collecting blood. But the IV stayed in my hand the entire time. My hand was sore and aching from it, and they didn't remove it until the very last day. The good thing was then at least I could move around and get closer to the baby when I wanted to!

I wish I could retell this story in a more joyful way. But every time I think about it, I just feel so sad. So many things went wrong. And I can't go back and fix it.
